claimText: According to the WHO Global TB Report 2025, India's TB incidence has reduced by 21%.
evidenceExcerpt: India's TB incidence reduced by 21% according to the WHO Global TB Report 2025.
examUse: Statement-based question on India's progress in disease elimination and recent WHO reports.
distractorHooks: Different report source (e.g., NITI Aayog Health Index)

claimText: Tuberculosis is an airborne disease caused by the bacterium Mycobacterium tuberculosis.
evidenceExcerpt: TB is an airborne disease caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis.
examUse: Statement-based question on the transmission mode and causative agent of TB.
distractorHooks: Viral or fungal pathogen instead of bacterial

claimText: Spinal Tuberculosis is also known as Pott's disease.
evidenceExcerpt: Spinal TB is also known as Pott's disease and affects the spine.
examUse: Correct-pair question matching diseases to their alternative names or specific affected organs.
distractorHooks: Claiming Pott's disease affects the lungs or brain

claimText: Multi Drug Resistant TB (MDR-TB) is a form of TB that does not respond to rifampicin and isoniazid, which are the two most effective first-line TB drugs.
evidenceExcerpt: Multi Drug Resistant TB (MDR-TB) does not respond to rifampicin and isoniazid, the two most effective first-line TB drugs.
examUse: Statement-based or comparison question distinguishing between drug-sensitive TB, MDR-TB, and XDR-TB.
distractorHooks: MDR-TB responds to isoniazid but not rifampicin
MDR-TB is resistant to second-line drugs but sensitive to first-line drugs

claimText: Extensively Drug-Resistant TB (XDR-TB) is a form of TB that resists both first-line drugs like isoniazid and rifampicin, as well as stronger second-line medicines.
evidenceExcerpt: MDR-TB does not respond to these two, while XDR-TB resists even stronger medicines.
examUse: Comparison question differentiating the severity and drug resistance profiles of MDR-TB and XDR-TB.
distractorHooks: XDR-TB can be easily treated with standard first-line drugs
XDR-TB only refers to resistance against a single second-line drug
